examples.title: Merge Two Sorted Linked Lists
examples.description: Iterative algorithm to merge two sorted singly linked lists into one sorted list
examples.code:
PROCEDURE MergeSortedLists(L1, L2)
    // Create dummy header for simplified edge case handling
    resultHead ← NEW Node()
    current ← resultHead
    
    // Traverse both lists, attaching smaller node each iteration
    WHILE (L1 ≠ NULL) AND (L2 ≠ NULL) DO
        IF (L1.data ≤ L2.data) THEN
            current.next ← L1
            L1 ← L1.next
        ELSE
            current.next ← L2
            L2 ← L2.next
        END IF
        current ← current.next
    END WHILE
    
    // Attach remaining list (no comparison needed)
    IF L1 ≠ NULL THEN
        current.next ← L1
    ELSE
        current.next ← L2
    END IF
    
    RETURN resultHead.next  // Skip dummy header
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: The algorithm uses a dummy header node to eliminate special handling for the first element. At each step, we compare current elements of both lists and append the smaller one to the result. When one list exhausts, we simply attach the remaining list directly without further comparisons. This achieves optimal O(m+n) time complexity.

examples.title: Reverse Linked List - Iterative
examples.description: In-place reversal of singly linked list using three-pointer technique
examples.explanation: The key insight is reversing the direction of each next pointer while preserving access to the next node via the temporary 'next' pointer. After processing all nodes, prev points to what was the last node (now the head of reversed list). This achieves in-place reversal without recursion or auxiliary data structures.

examples.title: Polynomial Addition Using Linked Lists
examples.description: Add two polynomials represented as linked lists with descending exponents
examples.code:
PROCEDURE AddPolynomials(P1, P2)
    result ← NULL
    resultTail ← NULL
    
    WHILE (P1 ≠ NULL) AND (P2 ≠ NULL) DO
        newNode ← NEW Node()
        
        IF P1.exponent = P2.exponent THEN
            // Combine like terms
            newNode.coefficient ← P1.coefficient + P2.coefficient
            newNode.exponent ← P1.exponent
            P1 ← P1.next
            P2 ← P2.next
        ELSE IF P1.exponent > P2.exponent THEN
            // P1 has higher exponent term
            newNode.coefficient ← P1.coefficient
            newNode.exponent ← P1.exponent
            P1 ← P1.next
        ELSE
            // P2 has higher exponent term
            newNode.coefficient ← P2.coefficient
            newNode.exponent ← P2.exponent
            P2 ← P2.next
        END IF
        
        // Append to result (skip if coefficient is zero)
        IF newNode.coefficient ≠ 0 THEN
            IF result = NULL THEN
                result ← newNode
                resultTail ← newNode
            ELSE
                resultTail.next ← newNode
                resultTail ← newNode
            END IF
        END IF
    END WHILE
    
    // Append remaining terms from non-exhausted polynomial
    remaining ← (P1 ≠ NULL) ? P1 : P2
    WHILE remaining ≠ NULL DO
        IF remaining.coefficient ≠ 0 THEN
            resultTail.next ← NEW Node(remaining.coefficient, remaining.exponent)
            resultTail ← resultTail.next
        END IF
        remaining ← remaining.next
    END WHILE
    
    RETURN result
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: Both input polynomials are sorted in descending order of exponents. We traverse simultaneously, comparing exponents at each step. Matching exponents get their coefficients added; the larger exponent gets copied directly. Any remaining terms after one list exhausts are appended as-is. This is essentially a merge operation with coefficient addition for equal exponents.
